project_goal: A collection of empirical economics and data science research projects analyzing monetary policy, fiscal dynamics, agent-based economic models, and energy policy using Python, Jupyter notebooks, statistical modeling, and interactive visualization. Each sub-project produces research-grade analysis paired with interactive HTML charts or data-driven whitepapers.

authority_sources.name: FRED / Federal Reserve Economic Data
authority_sources.scope: Federal Reserve monetary data fetch conventions and series codes

authority_sources.name: datlib library
authority_sources.scope: DAG construction, time-series testing, statistical analysis, and plotting conventions for the Fed DAG project

authority_sources.name: homebrewedFunctions library
authority_sources.scope: Shared helper functions for the Prairie Prosperity fiscal analysis

authority_sources.name: Crisis and Credit Allocation notebook
authority_sources.path: Crisis and Credit Allocation/Crisis and Credit Allocation Data.ipynb
authority_sources.scope: Canonical analysis script for crisis-era banking and credit data

authority_sources.name: Sugarscape model source
authority_sources.scope: Agent-based model implementation — Agent.py, Model.py, Patch.py are the authoritative model definitions

conversion_pipeline: jupyter nbconvert --to html <notebook>.ipynb --output-dir */Whitepaper/ (retain standalone Plotly HTML artifacts in */outputs/ without further conversion)
style_rules: Jupyter notebooks are the primary authoring surface; Python .py files are supporting libraries or model classes
Interactive HTML outputs use Plotly; static plots use matplotlib
Data files (CSV, Excel, parquet) are raw inputs — never modify source data in notebooks, only transform into derived frames
Each sub-project is self-contained in its own directory; cross-project imports are not permitted
Cite academic sources inline in notebook markdown cells using AuthorYear convention

components.name: Crisis and Credit Allocation
components.output_file: Crisis and Credit Allocation/Crisis and Credit Allocation Data.ipynb
components.description: Empirical analysis of banking credit allocation during major U.S. economic crises. Uses FRED API and Federal Reserve bank data (BankData.xlsx, fedData.csv) to construct normalized time-series comparisons across the Great Depression (1929), the 2008 Financial Crisis, and COVID-19 periods. Produces matplotlib figures showing loans-and-investments trends relative to pre-crisis baselines.
sections: Data ingestion from FRED and Excel sources
Normalization and time-series alignment across crisis episodes
Visualization of credit allocation divergence by crisis period
quality_criteria: All FRED series codes are documented in the notebook
Normalization baseline date is explicitly defined and reproducible
Figures include axis labels, legends, and crisis-period annotations

components.name: Federal Reserve Response Function DAG Analysis
components.description: Causal analysis of the Federal Reserve's monetary policy response function using directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). Builds on the custom datlib library (FRED.py, DAG.py, ts_tests.py, stats.py, plots.py) to fetch interest rate and monetary aggregate time-series from FRED, run stationarity and causality tests, construct DAG representations, and produce visualization outputs. Data serialized to parquet for reproducibility.
sections: FRED data fetch and parquet serialization (datlib.FRED)
quality_criteria: DAG edges are justified by prior economic theory or test results
Parquet snapshot matches the FRED series codes listed in the notebook

components.name: More Prairie Prosperity — Fiscal and Economic Policy in North Dakota
components.description: State-level fiscal and economic policy research for North Dakota combining panel regression analysis with interactive Plotly HTML reports and a narrative whitepaper. The MorePrairieProsperity directory contains the analysis notebooks and homebrewedFunctions library; the PropertyTaxes/Whitepaper directory contains the published whitepaper notebook (MorePrairieProsperity-EconomyAndFiscalPolicyInNorthDakota.ipynb) that embeds charts via iframes. Key methods: PanelOLS fixed-effects regression, R-squared dropdown figures, combined interactive HTML export.
sections: Data loading and normalization (state tax/income CSVs)
quality_criteria: All state-level CSV sources are documented in notebook
PanelOLS models report entity and time fixed-effects specification
Interactive HTML outputs load correctly without external dependencies

components.name: Sugarscape Agent-Based Model
components.description: Agent-based simulation comparing neoclassical (utility-maximizing) and heuristic-based agents in the Sugarscape environment (Epstein & Axtell 1996), with an optional arbitrageur agent. Implemented as an object-oriented Python model (Agent.py, Model.py, Patch.py) with a GUI entry point (GUI.py) and SQL-based data aggregation (DataAggregatorSQL.py). Outputs are stored to parquet for post-simulation analysis. Part of a master's thesis in applied economics at NDSU.
sections: Agent decision rules (neoclassical vs. heuristic, Agent.py)
quality_criteria: Agent breeds and decision rule variants are documented in readme
Simulation parameters (runs, periods, breed_sets) are configurable without code changes
SQL aggregation produces reproducible summary statistics

components.name: Visualize Energy Data
components.description: Interactive HTML visualizations analyzing electricity cost differentials across generation sources. Outputs include per-kWh cost charts, per-capita excess cost comparisons, total excess cost stacked gradient charts, and policy-scenario variants excluding offshore or onshore wind. All outputs are standalone HTML files using Plotly.
sections: Electricity cost data ingestion and processing
Stacked gradient chart generation for renewable policy scenarios
HTML export of all interactive figures
quality_criteria: Source data provenance is documented (EIA or similar)
Excess cost baseline is clearly defined and reproducible
All HTML outputs are self-contained (no external CDN dependencies)
